title: Who fact-checks the coconut wireless?
gist: A concentrated Pacific election cycle (New Caledonia, Cook Islands, Fiji, PNG through 2027) collides with generative AI that Fiji's already-degraded information environment can't withstand, and the proposed fix is trained community verification networks operating at disinformation's speed rather than top-down moderation.

insights.claim: The Pacific enters a concentrated electoral stretch — New Caledonia (June 2026), Cook Islands (12 August), Fiji (24 December 2026 to early February 2027) and Papua New Guinea (by mid-2027) — just as generative AI introduces threats the region's democratic infrastructure was not built to meet.
insights.speaker: Michaela Long
insights.so_what: A dense election calendar concentrates AI-disinformation risk across Pacific states over 18 months.
insights.locator: The Pacific is entering a concentrated stretch

insights.claim: Generative AI lets anyone with an internet connection produce content in iTaukei, Fiji Hindi or local dialects that reads as authentically Fijian, at a volume no Pacific newsroom or platform moderation team can match.
insights.speaker: Michaela Long
insights.so_what: Minority-language AI content outpaces platform moderation — a structural gap adversaries can exploit.
insights.locator: Generative AI extends this dynamic further

insights.claim: Fiji's 2010–2023 Media Industry Development Decree narrowed reporting on government and judiciary under penalty of fines and imprisonment, displacing audiences to social media and 'coconut wireless' so a generation learned to treat unverified information as a normal substitute for verified reporting.
insights.speaker: Michaela Long
insights.so_what: Media repression created the durable trust vacuum AI now exploits — a cautionary pattern for other states.
insights.locator: That history began during the Bainimarama government era

insights.claim: With few credible domestic news sources left to compete for trust, Facebook became the dominant source of news in Fiji.
insights.speaker: Michaela Long
insights.so_what: Platform dependence concentrates information risk on a single, weakly-moderated channel.
insights.locator: With few credible domestic news sources left

insights.claim: In July 2025 a deepfake video falsely depicted Fiji Television's news manager Stella Taoi endorsing a fraudulent investment scheme, illustrating AI-enabled impersonation weaponising a trusted journalist's face.
insights.speaker: Michaela Long
insights.so_what: Deepfake financial-scam impersonation is already live in the Pacific — a concrete fraud and trust risk.

insights.claim: Institutional verification works on checkable facts but cannot catch a bot account built and maintained over months — only a community member who knows the terrain can notice something is off.
insights.speaker: Michaela Long
insights.so_what: The hardest disinformation is behavioural, not factual — favours community-level detection over automated tools.
insights.locator: This matters most for what institutions are worst placed to catch

insights.claim: The proposed fix is trained community verification networks drawn from churches, women's groups and village councils feeding village-level rumour into Fiji's Elections Office–Online Safety Commission monitoring unit, operating at the speed disinformation does.
insights.speaker: Michaela Long
insights.so_what: A replicable Pacific model that bypasses slow platform moderation and treaty processes.
insights.locator: Establishing that capacity requires training community contacts

insights.claim: The December 2025 Fijian Elections Office–Online Safety Commission MOU covers AI-generated content but cannot reach the WhatsApp groups and hyper-localised newsfeeds where much disinformation actually circulates.
insights.speaker: Michaela Long
insights.so_what: Formal monitoring has a structural blind spot in closed messaging apps — the real battleground.
insights.locator: However, what the MOU cannot do
